# Workflow DevKit

Workflow DevKit is a durable functions framework for JavaScript/TypeScript that enables writing long-running, stateful application logic on top of stateless compute. The runtime persists progress as an event log and deterministically replays code to reconstruct state after cold starts, failures, or scale events. This allows workflows to survive infrastructure disruptions, scale horizontally, and run for extended periods without the constraints of traditional serverless function timeouts.

The framework uses a unique execution model with two types of functions: **workflow functions** (marked with `"use workflow"`) that orchestrate logic in a sandboxed VM, and **step functions** (marked with `"use step"`) that execute individual pieces of logic with full Node.js runtime access. The compiler automatically transforms workflow files into separate bundles for different execution contexts, enabling seamless integration with frameworks like Next.js, Nitro, Astro, SvelteKit, and more.

## Core APIs

### Defining a Workflow Function

Workflow functions are the orchestrators that coordinate step execution. They run in a sandboxed VM without full Node.js access but can call step functions for side effects.

```typescript
// workflows/order-processing.ts
import { sleep, createHook, FatalError } from 'workflow';

// Step function with full Node.js access
async function processPayment(orderId: string, amount: number) {
  'use step';

  const response = await fetch('https://api.stripe.com/v1/charges', {
    method: 'POST',
    headers: { 'Authorization': `Bearer ${process.env.STRIPE_KEY}` },
    body: JSON.stringify({ amount, currency: 'usd', source: orderId })
  });

  if (!response.ok) {
    throw new FatalError('Payment failed permanently');
  }

  return response.json();
}

async function sendConfirmationEmail(email: string, orderId: string) {
  'use step';

  await fetch('https://api.sendgrid.com/v3/mail/send', {
    method: 'POST',
    headers: { 'Authorization': `Bearer ${process.env.SENDGRID_KEY}` },
    body: JSON.stringify({
      to: email,
      subject: 'Order Confirmed',
      text: `Your order ${orderId} has been confirmed!`
    })
  });
}

// Workflow function that orchestrates the steps
export async function processOrder(orderId: string, email: string, amount: number) {
  'use workflow';

  console.log(`Processing order: ${orderId}`);

  // Each step is automatically retried on failure
  const payment = await processPayment(orderId, amount);
  console.log('Payment processed:', payment.id);

  // Sleep for 5 seconds (persisted, survives restarts)
  await sleep('5s');

  await sendConfirmationEmail(email, orderId);

  return { orderId, paymentId: payment.id, status: 'completed' };
}
```

### Starting a Workflow

The `start()` function initiates a workflow run and returns a `Run` object for tracking and managing the execution.

```typescript
// app/api/orders/route.ts
import { start, getRun } from 'workflow/api';
import { processOrder } from '@/workflows/order-processing';

export async function POST(request: Request) {
  const { orderId, email, amount } = await request.json();

  // Start the workflow - returns immediately
  const run = await start(processOrder, [orderId, email, amount]);

  return Response.json({
    runId: run.runId,
    message: 'Order processing started'
  });
}

export async function GET(request: Request) {
  const url = new URL(request.url);
  const runId = url.searchParams.get('runId');

  // Get an existing run by ID
  const run = getRun(runId!);

  // Check status without blocking
  const status = await run.status;

  if (status === 'completed') {
    // Get the return value (blocks until completion)
    const result = await run.returnValue;
    return Response.json({ status, result });
  }

  return Response.json({ status });
}
```

### Sleep Function

The `sleep()` function pauses workflow execution for a specified duration. The sleep is persisted, so the workflow can resume after infrastructure restarts.

```typescript
import { sleep } from 'workflow';

export async function scheduledReminder(userId: string, message: string) {
  'use workflow';

  // Sleep using duration string (supports ms, s, m, h, d)
  await sleep('30m');
  await sendNotification(userId, 'Reminder in 30 minutes!');

  // Sleep until a specific date
  const eventDate = new Date('2024-12-25T10:00:00Z');
  await sleep(eventDate);
  await sendNotification(userId, message);

  // Sleep using milliseconds
  await sleep(5000);

  return { delivered: true };
}

async function sendNotification(userId: string, message: string) {
  'use step';
  await fetch(`https://api.example.com/notify/${userId}`, {
    method: 'POST',
    body: JSON.stringify({ message })
  });
}
```

### Hooks for External Events

Hooks allow workflows to suspend and wait for external events. Use `createHook()` for server-side events or `createWebhook()` for HTTP-triggered events.

```typescript
import { createHook, createWebhook, resumeHook } from 'workflow';

// Workflow that waits for approval
export async function documentApproval(docId: string, approverEmail: string) {
  'use workflow';

  await sendApprovalRequest(docId, approverEmail);

  // Create a hook with a deterministic token
  using hook = createHook<{ approved: boolean; comments?: string }>({
    token: `approval:${docId}`,
    metadata: { docId, approverEmail }
  });

  console.log('Waiting for approval, token:', hook.token);

  // Workflow suspends here until hook is resumed
  const decision = await hook;

  if (decision.approved) {
    await publishDocument(docId);
    return { status: 'published' };
  }

  return { status: 'rejected', reason: decision.comments };
}

// Workflow with public webhook URL
export async function paymentCallback(orderId: string) {
  'use workflow';

  const webhook = createWebhook({
    respondWith: new Response('OK', { status: 200 })
  });

  // Send webhook URL to payment provider
  await registerPaymentCallback(orderId, webhook.url);

  // Wait for payment provider to call the webhook
  const request = await webhook;
  const payload = await request.json();

  return { orderId, paymentStatus: payload.status };
}

async function sendApprovalRequest(docId: string, email: string) {
  'use step';
  // Send email with approval link
}

async function publishDocument(docId: string) {
  'use step';
  // Publish the document
}

async function registerPaymentCallback(orderId: string, callbackUrl: string) {
  'use step';
  // Register callback with payment provider
}
```

### Resuming Hooks from Server Code

Use `resumeHook()` from API routes or server actions to send data to a waiting workflow.

```typescript
// app/api/approve/route.ts
import { resumeHook, getHookByToken, HookNotFoundError } from 'workflow/api';

export async function POST(request: Request) {
  const { token, approved, comments } = await request.json();

  try {
    // Get hook metadata before resuming (optional)
    const hook = await getHookByToken(token);
    console.log('Resuming hook for doc:', hook.metadata?.docId);

    // Resume the workflow with the approval decision
    await resumeHook(token, { approved, comments });

    return Response.json({ success: true, runId: hook.runId });
  } catch (error) {
    if (HookNotFoundError.is(error)) {
      return Response.json({ error: 'Hook not found or expired' }, { status: 404 });
    }
    throw error;
  }
}
```

### Error Handling

Use `FatalError` for non-retryable errors and `RetryableError` for configurable retry behavior.

```typescript
import { FatalError, RetryableError } from 'workflow';

async function processWithRetry(data: any) {
  'use step';

  try {
    const response = await fetch('https://api.example.com/process', {
      method: 'POST',
      body: JSON.stringify(data)
    });

    if (response.status === 429) {
      const retryAfter = response.headers.get('Retry-After');
      throw new RetryableError('Rate limited', {
        retryAfter: retryAfter ? `${retryAfter}s` : '30s'
      });
    }

    if (response.status === 400) {
      throw new FatalError('Invalid request - will not retry');
    }

    if (!response.ok) {
      // Regular errors are automatically retried with exponential backoff
      throw new Error(`Request failed: ${response.status}`);
    }

    return response.json();
  } catch (error) {
    if (error instanceof TypeError) {
      // Network errors - retry after 5 seconds
      throw new RetryableError('Network error', { retryAfter: '5s' });
    }
    throw error;
  }
}

export async function reliableProcessing(items: any[]) {
  'use workflow';

  const results = [];

  for (const item of items) {
    try {
      const result = await processWithRetry(item);
      results.push({ success: true, data: result });
    } catch (error) {
      // FatalError propagates to workflow, allowing graceful handling
      if (FatalError.is(error)) {
        results.push({ success: false, error: error.message });
        continue;
      }
      throw error;
    }
  }

  return results;
}
```

### Streaming Data from Steps

Use `getWritable()` to stream data from step functions that can be read outside the workflow.

```typescript
import { getWritable, getRun, start } from 'workflow';

async function streamProgress(items: string[]) {
  'use step';

  const writable = getWritable<{ progress: number; item: string }>();
  const writer = writable.getWriter();

  for (let i = 0; i < items.length; i++) {
    await processItem(items[i]);
    await writer.write({
      progress: ((i + 1) / items.length) * 100,
      item: items[i]
    });
  }

  await writer.close();
}

async function processItem(item: string) {
  // Process logic
}

export async function batchProcess(items: string[]) {
  'use workflow';
  await streamProgress(items);
  return { processed: items.length };
}

// Reading the stream from an API route
export async function GET(request: Request) {
  const url = new URL(request.url);
  const runId = url.searchParams.get('runId');

  const run = getRun(runId!);
  const readable = run.getReadable<{ progress: number; item: string }>();

  return new Response(
    readable.pipeThrough(new TransformStream({
      transform(chunk, controller) {
        controller.enqueue(`data: ${JSON.stringify(chunk)}\n\n`);
      }
    })),
    { headers: { 'Content-Type': 'text/event-stream' } }
  );
}
```

### Run Management

The `Run` class provides methods for monitoring and controlling workflow execution.

```typescript
import { start, getRun, WorkflowRunFailedError, WorkflowRunCancelledError } from 'workflow/api';
import { myWorkflow } from '@/workflows/my-workflow';

async function manageWorkflow() {
  // Start a new workflow
  const run = await start(myWorkflow, ['arg1', 'arg2']);
  console.log('Started run:', run.runId);

  // Check if run exists
  const exists = await run.exists;

  // Get run metadata
  const workflowName = await run.workflowName;
  const createdAt = await run.createdAt;
  const startedAt = await run.startedAt;

  // Poll status
  const status = await run.status; // 'pending' | 'running' | 'completed' | 'failed' | 'cancelled'

  // Cancel a running workflow
  if (status === 'running') {
    await run.cancel();
  }

  // Wake up a sleeping workflow early
  const result = await run.wakeUp();
  console.log('Interrupted sleeps:', result.count);

  // Wait for completion and get result
  try {
    const returnValue = await run.returnValue;
    console.log('Workflow completed:', returnValue);
  } catch (error) {
    if (WorkflowRunFailedError.is(error)) {
      console.error('Workflow failed:', error.cause.message);
    } else if (WorkflowRunCancelledError.is(error)) {
      console.log('Workflow was cancelled');
    }
  }
}

// Get an existing run by ID
const existingRun = getRun<{ result: string }>('wrun_01HXYZ...');
const result = await existingRun.returnValue;
```

### Next.js Integration

Configure Next.js with the `withWorkflow` wrapper in your next.config.js.

```javascript
// next.config.js
import { withWorkflow } from 'workflow/next';

export default withWorkflow({
  // Your existing Next.js config
  experimental: {
    // ...
  }
}, {
  workflows: {
    // Optional: Enable lazy discovery for faster dev server startup
    lazyDiscovery: true,
    // Optional: Configure local development
    local: {
      port: 3000,
      dataDir: '.next/workflow-data'
    }
  }
});
```

### AI Integration with Vercel AI SDK

Workflow DevKit integrates seamlessly with the Vercel AI SDK for building durable AI agents.

```typescript
import { generateText, stepCountIs } from 'ai';
import z from 'zod';

async function searchDatabase(query: string) {
  'use step';
  // Database search with full Node.js access
  const results = await db.search(query);
  return results;
}

async function sendEmail(to: string, subject: string, body: string) {
  'use step';
  await emailService.send({ to, subject, body });
  return { sent: true };
}

export async function aiAssistant(userMessage: string) {
  'use workflow';

  // AI SDK's generateText works natively in workflows
  const { text } = await generateText({
    model: 'anthropic/claude-3-sonnet',
    prompt: userMessage,
    tools: {
      searchDatabase: {
        description: 'Search the knowledge base',
        inputSchema: z.object({ query: z.string() }),
        execute: searchDatabase,
      },
      sendEmail: {
        description: 'Send an email to a user',
        inputSchema: z.object({
          to: z.string().email(),
          subject: z.string(),
          body: z.string(),
        }),
        execute: sendEmail,
      },
    },
    // Allow up to 10 tool calls (no lambda timeout restrictions)
    stopWhen: stepCountIs(10),
  });

  return { response: text };
}
```

### WorkflowChatTransport for Resumable Chat

Use `WorkflowChatTransport` for building chat interfaces with automatic reconnection support.

```typescript
// Client-side chat component
import { useChat } from 'ai/react';
import { WorkflowChatTransport } from '@workflow/ai';

const transport = new WorkflowChatTransport({
  api: '/api/chat',
  maxConsecutiveErrors: 3,
  initialStartIndex: -10, // Resume from last 10 messages on reconnect
  onChatEnd: ({ chatId, chunkIndex }) => {
    console.log(`Chat ${chatId} ended after ${chunkIndex} chunks`);
  }
});

function ChatComponent() {
  const { messages, input, handleSubmit, setInput } = useChat({
    transport,
    id: 'my-chat-session',
  });

  return (
    <div>
      {messages.map(m => (
        <div key={m.id}>{m.content}</div>
      ))}
      <form onSubmit={handleSubmit}>
        <input value={input} onChange={e => setInput(e.target.value)} />
        <button type="submit">Send</button>
      </form>
    </div>
  );
}
```

### Testing with Vitest

Use `@workflow/vitest` for testing workflows in isolation with a local world.

```typescript
// vitest.config.ts
import { workflow } from '@workflow/vitest';
import { defineConfig } from 'vitest/config';

export default defineConfig({
  plugins: [workflow()],
});

// __tests__/workflows.test.ts
import { describe, it, expect, beforeAll, afterAll } from 'vitest';
import { start, resumeHook } from 'workflow/api';
import { waitForHook, waitForSleep, setupWorkflowTests, teardownWorkflowTests } from '@workflow/vitest';
import { documentApproval } from '@/workflows/approval';

describe('Document Approval Workflow', () => {
  beforeAll(async () => {
    await setupWorkflowTests();
  });

  afterAll(async () => {
    await teardownWorkflowTests();
  });

  it('should publish document when approved', async () => {
    const run = await start(documentApproval, ['doc-123', 'approver@example.com']);

    // Wait for the workflow to create a hook
    const hook = await waitForHook(run, { timeout: 5000 });
    expect(hook.token).toBe('approval:doc-123');

    // Resume the hook with approval
    await resumeHook(hook.token, { approved: true });

    // Wait for completion
    const result = await run.returnValue;
    expect(result.status).toBe('published');
  });

  it('should reject document with comments', async () => {
    const run = await start(documentApproval, ['doc-456', 'approver@example.com']);

    const hook = await waitForHook(run);
    await resumeHook(hook.token, {
      approved: false,
      comments: 'Needs revision'
    });

    const result = await run.returnValue;
    expect(result.status).toBe('rejected');
    expect(result.reason).toBe('Needs revision');
  });

  it('should handle sleep correctly', async () => {
    const run = await start(scheduledWorkflow, []);

    // Wait for workflow to hit sleep
    const sleepId = await waitForSleep(run);

    // Wake up the workflow early
    await run.wakeUp({ correlationIds: [sleepId] });

    const result = await run.returnValue;
    expect(result.completed).toBe(true);
  });
});
```

### CLI Commands

The Workflow CLI provides commands for development and debugging.

```bash
# Initialize a new workflow project
workflow init

# Start the development server
workflow dev

# Build workflow bundles
workflow build

# Start a workflow run manually
workflow start <workflow-name> [args...]
wf start processOrder '"order-123"' '"user@email.com"' 99.99

# Inspect a running workflow
workflow inspect <run-id>

# Cancel a workflow run
workflow cancel <run-id>

# Check workflow health
workflow health

# Validate workflow configuration
workflow validate
```

## Summary

Workflow DevKit enables building resilient, long-running applications by combining the simplicity of regular async/await code with durable execution guarantees. The framework handles state persistence, automatic retries, and deterministic replay transparently, allowing developers to focus on business logic rather than infrastructure concerns.

The key integration patterns include: using `"use workflow"` and `"use step"` directives to define durable functions, employing hooks and webhooks for external event coordination, leveraging the AI SDK integration for building intelligent agents, and utilizing the testing utilities for comprehensive workflow validation. With support for multiple frameworks (Next.js, Nitro, Astro, SvelteKit, NestJS) and seamless Vercel deployment, Workflow DevKit provides a production-ready foundation for building sophisticated stateful applications.
